questions.question: Explain the Vanishing Gradient Problem in Deep Neural Networks. Why is it more severe in deeper networks?
keyPoints: Gradients become exponentially small during backpropagation
Each layer multiplies gradient by derivative of activation (<1 for sigmoid/tanh)
Deep networks multiply many small values, making early layer gradients approach zero
Early layers learn very slowly or stop learning entirely

questions.question: What is Batch Normalization? Explain any two benefits of using Batch Normalization in deep networks.
keyPoints: Normalizes layer inputs to zero mean and unit variance
Reduces internal covariate shift
Allows higher learning rates
Has slight regularization effect
Reduces dependence on careful initialization

questions.question: Differentiate between Xavier and He initialization. When would you use each?
keyPoints: Xavier: variance = 2/(n_in + n_out) for tanh/sigmoid
He: variance = 2/n_in specifically for ReLU
Xavier accounts for both input and output neurons
He accounts for ReLU zeroing half the signal
Use Xavier for tanh/sigmoid, He for ReLU activations

questions.question: What are Skip Connections in Deep Neural Networks? How do they help in training very deep networks?
keyPoints: Connections that bypass one or more layers: Output = F(x) + x
Creates gradient highway for backpropagation
Enables gradient to flow directly to earlier layers
Solves vanishing gradient problem in very deep networks
Enabled successful training of 100+ layer networks (ResNet)

questions.question: What is the Dying ReLU problem? How can it be solved?
keyPoints: ReLU neurons stuck outputting zero for all inputs
Occurs when weights cause all inputs to be negative
Gradient is zero for negative inputs, so neurons never recover
Solutions: Leaky ReLU, Parametric ReLU (PReLU), ELU
These allow small non-zero gradient for negative inputs

questions.question: Explain the Exploding Gradient Problem. How does gradient clipping help?
keyPoints: Gradients become exponentially large during backpropagation
Caused by poor initialization or very deep networks
Makes training unstable, loss diverges
Gradient clipping caps gradient magnitude at a threshold
Preserves direction while limiting magnitude

questions.question: What is Internal Covariate Shift? How does Batch Normalization address this problem?
keyPoints: Change in distribution of layer activations during training
Each layer's input distribution shifts as previous layers update
Makes training slower, requires smaller learning rates
Batch Norm normalizes inputs to fixed mean and variance
Reduces dependency between layers, stabilizes training

questions.question: Discuss the various challenges faced when training Deep Neural Networks. Explain at least four techniques used to address these challenges.
keyPoints: Vanishing/Exploding gradients - addressed by proper initialization, ReLU, skip connections
Internal covariate shift - addressed by Batch Normalization
Optimization difficulty in non-convex landscape - addressed by Adam, proper LR
Dying ReLU - addressed by Leaky ReLU, ELU
Need to mention at least 4 techniques with explanations

questions.question: Explain the concept of Residual Networks (ResNet). How do skip connections enable training of very deep networks? Provide mathematical justification.
keyPoints: ResNet uses skip connections: H(x) = F(x) + x
Enables gradient to flow directly to earlier layers during backpropagation
Without skip: gradient multiplies through all layers
With skip: gradient has direct path, avoiding multiplication
Mathematical: dL/dx = dL/dH * (dF/dx + 1)
This preserves gradient even for very deep networks
Enabled successful training of 100+ layer networks

questions.question: Compare Batch Normalization, Layer Normalization, and Instance Normalization. When would you use each?
keyPoints: Batch Norm: normalizes across batch dimension, requires sufficient batch size
Layer Norm: normalizes across features for each sample, works for RNNs
Instance Norm: normalizes per sample per channel, used in style transfer
Batch: most common for CNNs, requires larger batches
Layer: better for RNNs, variable batch sizes
Instance: specifically for image style transfer tasks

questions.question: Explain the importance of weight initialization in training Deep Neural Networks. Derive the formula for Xavier initialization and explain why it differs for sigmoid/tanh and ReLU activations.
keyPoints: Poor initialization can cause vanishing/exploding gradients
Goal: maintain variance of activations across layers
Xavier derivation: Var(W) = 2/(n_in + n_out) for symmetric activations
For sigmoid/tanh: accounts for both forward and backward signal
For ReLU: He initialization uses 2/n_in because ReLU zeros half the signal
Wrong initialization leads to either vanishing or exploding activations
